sentences.text: That is a question which cannot actually be answered in exact detail.
I have a document, a [INAUDIBLE] publication, which talks about 12,000 Russians being in the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would just leave it to this, that there were vast numbers and it ran into thousands.
And they were from-- there were French, Belgian, Dutch, Italian, German-- there was even a handful of Germans.
Polish, Danish, Norwegian, about whom I will speak more later, Russians, Ukrainians, Hungarians, and from Southeastern Europe.
They were all there.
There were no-- no Swiss, of course.
We had one American.
And he said he'd been in Neuengamme.
My-- my colonel looked at him.
He immediately ordered him into close arrest, because he was so fat.
He said, well, if he was in Neuengamme as a prisoner, he wouldn't looked as well as that.
So he notified the American authorities.
And the American authorities came and took him away.
And I never saw him again.
How many of them were Jews?
Again, it's impossible to say.
Because as I've said before, there was no Israeli state.
And they were known as Polish Jews, French Jews, Dutch Jews.
For instance, [?
Hubert ?] [?
Steinberg ?] was a Dutch Jew.
Quite a lot of them were Jews.
But I would split up the Russians.
I don't know because there-- there were vast hoards of them.
But there were a lot of Jews there.
And I found them very nice people to deal with.
Oh, well, they had gone through hell.
They had-- they'd been lined up time and time again, as far as I can see, to be either moved under appalling circumstances-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were certainly hungry.
They had nothing to eat.
They'd had nothing to drink.
And some of them had been in saltwater, so you know what that means.
And-- oh, it was awful.
I remember one lady.
She was Polish.
And I think she was a Jew.
But I remember, she had a suckling baby.
And she wore an old brown coat.
And I told my escort to take her to the cookhouse and give her something to eat, and give her some milk for the baby.
And, anyway, I went up to her and chatted to her.
And she was covered with sores all on the tops of her breasts.
But she clearly had no other clothes on, except this old brown coat.
And she had no shoes on her feet or anything.
Anyway, I took an interest in that old lady.
And one morning, my escort was waiting for me at the officers' mess.
And he said, the Polish lady is waiting for you outside the office.
So I said, what's she want this time.
He said, she wants to say thank you.
I thought it was rather nice.
I didn't really-- some of them, I imagined had only just arrived there, you know, a matter of a day or two before.
They hadn't been there all that time.
You know, it-- it-- it-- it had no history as a concentration camp.
And I think it's-- because it was talked about as the Neustadt concentration camp.
But it wasn't a concentration camp.
It was, in fact, a Kriegsmarine training center.
And these poor people were pushed there, I'm sure.
Because there was nowhere else convenient in the town, when they came off the barges and various other places.
The-- but of course, they were still in-- where they had clothes, uniform-- that had this concentration camp stuff, the filthy blue and white striped trousers and jacket and skirts and hats.
And some of them still had found the-- the whips which their oppressors had used for them.
And I remember being shown one of this.
And I said, what happened to the chap who had it?
And he went, well, you know what that means.

The-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] my abiding memories are, as I went around the various buildings where we put these people in and we centralized the Dutch together-- and they would-- the Dutch from Groningen-- I remember a group of Dutch doctors.
And they were-- they were carcasses.
They're living carcasses.
And-- but when I went into their hut, as far as they could, they'd kept its-- they'd got it clean.
And this group of doctors wanted a picture of Queen Wilhelmina, the Dutch queen, and Montgomery.
And believe it or believe it not, I couldn't find a photograph of Montgomery, which is saying something.
Because he liked publicity.
But I found a photograph of our royal family.
And I took it along to them.
And they put it up there.
And they-- they were as pleased with that photograph of our king and queen and the two princesses as anything.
The-- some of them in-- when we actually went in on the 3rd of May, I remember in the shed, which I'll show you a photograph of, which we cleaned up and actually had a ceremonial parade there.
But they were lying there in this excreta.
Dreadful mess.
Dreadful mess.
And couldn't help themselves.
And in the huts where they lay, you know, their-- their bowels reacted.
It was terrible.
Dreadful.
Mind you, my men weren't very happy about it, either.
The men were very, very, very angry.
Well, I don't know.
I couldn't tell you.
I-- I-- all I can actually say, truthfully, is what I knew when I arrived.
Certainly, the armored car chap hadn't been there very long.
Matter of a couple of hours, I think, at the most.
The-- I understand that some people said they stayed there until they found something better, twelve months later.
They actually stayed in the camp.
Oh, I don't know.
I can't tell you.
I-- I just don't know.
The-- as far as that's concerned, there were numbers of people who had to be looked after medically, from hygiene, clothing, food, registered, and I-- I didn't ask people when they first went there.
The-- people were asked their name, where they came from, and-- we listed those.
But we didn't ask them how long they'd been there.
No.
No, no.
We didn't know.
I-- I-- I doubt if it had been weeks.
But I don't know.
Because I wasn't there.
Some had come from Neuengamme, which was a concentration camp, slave labor camp, which was near Hamburg.
And some were from Stutthof, which was near Danzig.
As a matter of fact, I know one lady who's living today in Melbourne who was, in fact, from Auschwitz and Stutthof, who was there when we liberated the camp on the 3rd of May.

At the-- when I-- on the very first day, when I went around the camp and I saw the size of the job, and knowing how many men I had, I knew that I couldn't cope and do a proper job, bearing in mind that we had to contain the people in the camp because of the threat of typhus.
And during the war, I'd worked with Norwegians and had Norwegians attached to me who had escaped from Norway.
And when I went into some of the accommodation and saw the Norwegians, I had the idea that I would seek their help.
And I could give them one job and give it to their officers to control it.
And that was to seal the camp on one side, across the isthmus, to keep the people in and to keep people out.
And I would arm them.
So I got a hold of the leader of the Norwegians.
His name was Andreas Orgens.
And I ask him how many men he had who were fit and capable of carrying arms and shooting if needs be.
And he said, about 250.
So I ask him if he would take on this job.
And he said, well, can I go and have a look at it?
So I took him along, and I said, well, this is the line I want you to occupy.
Because you can see from one end to the other.
And they'll be told, that-- you know, they don't cross it.
We'll put down wire.
They don't cross it.
And anybody tries to cross it, put a [INAUDIBLE] up their backside.
And, anyway, he took the job on.
And he did a great job.
But that was the only major help I got, other than two officers from my own regiment.
My colonel gave me a captain, whose name was Cohen.
And there's no prizes for guessing what his nationality was.
Vincent Cohen.
He-- I put him in charge of all the messing.
And he-- he was wonderful.
And then he gave me another subaltern then, Davis.
And he helped Vincent.
But, Vincent was my Christian Jew I told you about.
And he used to join us.
He was a good chap.
A very good officer.
Well, on the first day, it was very difficult.
Very difficult.
Because-- well, we weren't trained for it, as you can well imagine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very easy to blow one's top.
But we had to establish law and order.
And I was absolutely determined that in that camp, the British law and order would reign.
Now, the Russians, for instance, didn't see it that way.
They wanted an independent Russia in the camp.
And I said, no, you won't have it.
And they had an appointed head.
And there was only going to be one law and order there and that was British.
And that was fair to everybody, including the Germans.
And-- as I insisted that that was it.
And I was very wise, in retrospect, to have done it.
Because it curtailed people from settling things their own way.
And we-- we had trouble.
I mean, we-- the Russians decided they were going to fillet one bloak alive, who they said had been collaborating.
And he was brought into my office.
And the skin on his back was hanging down.
They were filleting him.
And that was one of their own people, mind you.
And anyway, we bundled him off to hospital and created hell with the leader.
I gave him the drubbing he would never forget.
But he was a good chap.
He was a-- a Russian tank corps officer.
Very good chap.
But when he-- when he realized that I wasn't going to stand for it, he supported me all the way down the road.
Until we got a Russian LO appointed in.
And the Russians sent us in an LO.
And he was a shocker.
But we threw him out of the camp.
And I think he got the roughest treatment of anybody.
He-- he-- he was purely and simply a politician.
I don't know.
We called him redbeard because he had a red beard.
But he spoke very good English.
But he wanted-- he-- he accused us-- me-- he accused us of letting the Germans poison his people.
And, well, I said, well, that's a load of rubbish-- that's not quite what I said-- but I said, the Russians-- the Germans are eating from the kitchen.
They're not going to poison that food.
Because they're eating it.
This is my guarantee.
And he said, well, I want the Russians to do it.
And I'm ordering you to do it.
I said, you can't issue any orders here, mate.
This is the British Army.
And eventually I threw him out of the camp.
Oh, well, they were very, very appreciative.
And I-- we-- we found, in this camp, a theater.
And we found pianos and things like that.
And among these wretched prisoners, we found very accomplished musicians.
One was the conductor of the Prague-- I think it was the Prague Philharmonic Orchestra.
Another one was-- her name was [?
Dora Rabinovich. ?] And she came, I think, from Lithuania.
She was a Lithuanian Jew, you know?
And she played.
Another one was a Polish lady.
Oh, they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] embarrassingly grateful.
I think that's probably the best way of putting it.
And-- but of course, the great thing when-- when we came around to clothing them.
We put a clothing levy on the town.
And then we had to distribute this clothing among these people.
And my second in command, who had left me at the time of the [INAUDIBLE] battle-- his father had died and he had to go home, cope with the family.
But he got back to the camp.
And he met-- he came into-- he actually arrived on VE night, which I think was the 7th or 8th of May.
And he took charge of all this distributing of clothes and all that.
And we decided that because the Red Army had fought this great war-- themselves, largely-- that the Russians would be clothed first.
So we got all these clothes in from the town.
And then I insisted that all the leading ladies in the town, parsons' wives-- I can't say rabbis' wives, but-- because there wasn't any rabbis.
But you know what I mean.
Doctors' wives, school teachers, and all those-- all had to come in.
And they distributed these clothes, which in fact had been theirs, to these poor people.
And they didn't like it.
Because when they came in, you see, they-- when they went out again, they had to be disinfected for typhus.
They're very hygienic, the Germans, you know.
And anyway, I handed over the distribution of these clothes to my second in command, [INAUDIBLE] Digby [?
Ames. ?] And Digby was very good.
Very good.
He's a real British Marshall figure, you know, military moustache, and-- stood about 6 foot 3.
And anyway, the-- put the Russians in to [INAUDIBLE] first-- let the Russian women-- women and children first.
And the first of those would be the Russians.
The dear old Russian woman was the first one that we kitted out.
And we gave her clothes, and shoes, and everything else.
Her hands-- obviously, I saw it-- her hands were absolutely wrinkled, you know, years of slaving-- she was a slave laborer, of course, in Germany.
And, anyway, she muttered something.
And I said to the Russian chap who we put in charge of it-- we used to call him [NON-ENGLISH], I said, what's she saying?
She says, she's saying thank you.
[LAUGHS] How else did they thank you?
Oh, embarrassingly, put on dances.
They'd dance national dances, you know, the-- but generally speaking, they were-- just wanted to be left alone, get their bodies clean, get some clothes on, and establish communication with somebody.
But they-- they smiled at you.
And they-- you know, they chatted away.
Well, I was very proud of the British army.
Because after all's said and done, we weren't trained for this sort of thing.
We did a good job.
Intensely proud of the-- the-- the whole setup.
I mean, I asked for the typhus equipment to come.
It just came.
Food.
Well, put the levy on the town for the first meals.
But, within a day, Red Cross parcels came, which had been lying in depots.
And we broke this bulk down.
And of course, then there was cigarettes to give the men.
The men that could have cigarettes.
Sounds a bit silly today, doesn't it?
There were sweets, boiled sweets for children.
Because these Red Cross parcels were-- came from the Red Cross of Canada, United Kingdom, America, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa.
But nothing from Russia, of course.
But you'd understand that.
The country had been scorched.
But, really was-- I was very proud.
Well, they were buried or taken away.
They were buried or taken away.
But I had nothing to do with that.
The-- The--
They were excavated where they'd been buried, where they'd been murdered.
They were excavated.
But that was outside the camp.
And I had so much in the camp.
That was much as I could cope with.
I talked about the-- the railway to Red Cross railway trains.
Well, I had nothing to do with those.
I had all I could cope with in the camp.
Oh, god knows.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I don't know.
Couldn't tell you.
I-- I saw one hut full.
And when you went through speaking to them, if they weren't dead, they were on their last legs.
Very sad.
But I didn't-- I was past it.
You see, [INAUDIBLE] living you-- are trying to save life.
And-- but the-- you see, there was a lot of bodies which were washed up at sea.
But again, it was outside my patch.
We had a German hospital in the camp.
And several of the leaders of the national group suggested that SS men might be sheltering in this German hospital.
You know, it wasn't a-- it was a emergency hospital.
One of the blocks, one of the permanent blocks, was being used for hospital purposes.
Well, we did that.
And I remember going to the hospital.
And, of course, they were fed from my kitchens.
And I saw the-- she was a Red Cross lady in charge of it.
And if she could have killed me, she would have killed me 50 times over.
She obviously hated my guts.
And-- although she wore the Red Cross.
And I said [INAUDIBLE] your ration strength.
And building.
Where are all these people?
Where are they all?
How many have been paid?
And we challenged our ration strength.
And she didn't like it, you know.
She felt that hospitals should be free from military intervention.
But, of course, it was inside the camp.
And we had to feed them.
And the-- we searched-- I-- I-- I put the leaders of the groups in-- I said, well, go through the hospital and see if you can find anybody.
If you want to pull the clothes down and check up, and see if they've got an SS tattoo here, do it.
They-- they started this bloody war and we've got to finish it.
And there was one other thing, which-- you mentioned, how did they express themselves, these people.
One or two of them used to come and play to us, play the piano.
And I had an officer who I put in charge of all the entertainment in the camp.
Not their national dancing and that sort of thing.
But we put on cinema shows.
Because the only films we could get were German ones at the time.
But we-- we caught them.
And one day, the officer in charge, his name was Peter [?
Lodge. ?] And he was the only regular officer in the battery.
And he said, I found a pianist, plays beautifully.
Can I bring her in the mess?
Because we-- we occupied the commandant's house.
And his Bechstein grand piano was still in the mess.
And I said, yeah.
Bring her in after supper.
And tell her she-- we'll give her something to eat.
And so I says, you get her organized, Peter.
Anyway, you bear in mind, she was a member-- inmate.
And when we got there, she had just had this blue and white uniform.
So anyway, we had our supper that night.
And Peter went off to fetch her, to bring her along at the mess to play to us.
And we knew she was Polish, at least we understood she was Polish.
Actually, in fact, I think she turned out to be Lithuanian.
But a lot of people were Polish if they came from that area.
And she came in.
And she was wearing a pair of Kriegsmarine trousers and one of my battery's football shirts.

Now, you know the slip which Collingwood use-- black and white strip-- well, that's the same strip as we used to use, except it was, you know, black and white all the way around, not like Collingwood, with a pattern on it.
And she came in wearing this.
And we all looked.
And Peter said, we'll find her some better clothes next time.
Anyway, she came in.
And she sat at this grand piano.
And we offered her a drink and a sandwich.
And she had a drink and a sandwich.
And she sat down.
And she put her fingers on this piano.
And we were just sitting around.
[HUMMING]
Well, anyway, she played it.
It was beautiful.
Anyway, she suddenly just flopped, held up her fingers, and said, they won't work.
They won't work.
Anyway, we gave her a chap and a cheer and shouted encore.
And then she had another go.
And then she played the-- oh [INAUDIBLE] my tongue.
It's Chopin.
I'll think of it later.
But she played a second bit.
Anyway, the next time, when she came and played the mess, she was dressed, you know, rather more formally.
But, it was very nice.
And that-- carrying on all the time, people would do that for you.
You'd go into a hut and people were obviously very pleased to see you.
But I felt sorry for the Norwegians.
Because Norway wasn't free.
But they were, in fact, the first people to go home.
I got a message to say that Swedish Red Cross column was coming in to take them home.
And so, that was the end of my help with the sealing off the camp.
And I gave the boss of the Norwegians, Andreas Orgens, a bottle of whisky.
And I autographed the label.
And I said, now, there you are.
You-- this is the real McCoy.
This is Scotch whisky.
Ration it out very carefully.
But keep the empty bottle.
Because I will come to Norway one day and I shall hope I'll see you and the empty bottle.
Anyway, I went about-- 9, 10 years later, I went on winter maneuvers.
And I called on him and his family.
And the little boy who had been born since his dad got home.
And Andreas said to me, I've got something I want to show you.
And he produced this empty bottle.
And he said, I've looked after it very carefully.
Now I have great pleasure in giving it to you.
I said, no.
I don't want it.
You keep it.
I said, it's empty.
It's no good to me.
[LAUGHS] Yes.
Oh.
But then, of course, at the end of it all, at the end of it, when we were told we were coming home, we had a big parade in the sports ground.
And I can show you photographs of that.
And then we eventually handed over to a commando.
And what happened, I just don't know, except what I wrote in my story.
And what I've told you on this tape is all down in writing, and much more.
I reckon that's the end of my story, except for the photographs.
